I'm good.
I'm good.

Yes.

ST like just a couple of months ago.
actually just moved back from New York.

but cool.
Well, thank you.
Sorry, I'm a little scattered this morning.
Tuesday mornings can be a little bit nuts, but very excited to chat about this role.

So it sounds like you've spoken with Tucker.
I can give you a bit of an overview of how I'm thinking about the role.
We'll talk about you, and then I actually have a hard stop at the...
11 and 30 minutes, so we'll keep it pretty short, but it doesn't, it usually, these conversations can be pretty quick.

Yeah, so growthx operates today in an agency model, so we have a pod structure, we're building like organic blog type content, so we're not doing like paid socials or video or anything like that, except in very rare cases or like for custom clients.
And so the pods are made up of a managing editor or a content manager who is in our internal platform generating content.

So while we are operating in an agency structure, we do have an internal platform where we've built all of our AI workflows, and that's what actually generates the first draft of all of the content that we produce.
So you would be kind of the project manager.
manager and main point of contact.
Certainly think of it as a customer success type role, where you're making sure that like we're on track for day-to-day deliverables, meeting our contractual obligations.
But the managing editor will be drafting the content and actually like putting it together.
And then you'll work with an account director who leads the high-level like strategy, long-term like goal and relationship alignment for clients.
So I think today you'd be working with like three to five clients.
And then the managing editor would have like kind of three pods under their purview.
Yeah.
Any questions?
No, I think it's quite a straightforward approach so far.
I have been in agency roles as well as on the SaaS side of things.
So I do have CSM experiences.
As well as agency, I've done SEO for the last few years as well.
So I think definitely this role is pretty interesting to me.
It's like right up my alley as well.
It sounds like it.
Cool.
Do you want to give me like your elevator pitch, quick overview?
Yeah, for sure.
So I have been in the SEO industry for about seven to eight years now.
Started off at a big agency, moved to a boutique agency.
So I was at iProspect before.
And then I moved to a smaller agency for a couple of months.
And then in the last four years, I've been doing, I've been working as SEO success manager.
So basically, in my previous role, we were doing a lot of like client management stuff.
We had a platform as well, my previous company, where I was the platform expert.
I had to, you know, onboard clients, make sure they are trained on the platform.
We've retained them.
So I guess it's a bit different from the role that you currently describe where, you know, it's more of a project management kind of thing where I'm not really.
that much in the platform.
But yeah, in my previous role, I was basically like the platform expert.
had to, yeah, just make sure that clients are really familiar with the platform.
And then we make sure that they're happy with the platform and all.
So I would say that, yeah, I mean, it's been a pretty fun, like, eight, nine years in the SEO space.
It's definitely changing a lot, especially with like, all the AI search engines that are coming up.
In my previous role as well, we did have some experience with that.
I would say that when I left, it was still kind of developing.
AI, I mean, AI search engines are also really new.
So it's a bit hard to be like an expert in that sense.
So it's just like, you know, we were learning along the way as well.
But, you know, we did have our own AI internal tools as well, AI workflows, how to manage them.
So yeah, pretty familiar with all these kinds of like, concepts and workflows.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Awesome.
Um, I am curious, um, can you tell me, uh, about, like, the teams that you worked with in the, in your roles?
Um, have you worked, like,-functionally or, um, for different projects?
Yeah, for sure.
So I worked a lot with, I would say, the product team in my previous role.
I worked a lot with the product team as well as, um, my project managers, um, as well as the solutions consultants.
So we worked, um, I did work a bit with sales.
So usually when, when a project is under, like, a POC, um, then we will work with the sales team with the solutions consultant.

But most of the time, I would say the people that I work closest with, um, are definitely, um, our product, like, product managers, the product team, just to make sure that, you know, we are pushing out the right kind of product for, um, the clients as well and making sure their needs are met on the platform.
Awesome.
Can you give me an, like a specific example of a client where you've had to support with, teams?
You've had to support with across teams.
Yeah, for sure.
So I, so one of my clients, when I first moved to the US, I kind of like took over this account.
They were like a complete mess.
They were like, oh, your product doesn't work.
It's like, you know, it's really slow, blah, blah, blah.
Like, so it was like on fire and I took over that account.
Basically what we had to do was just like to align, like, you know, their needs, their strategies.
Just basically making sure that their needs kind of like align with what we can provide within, in the platform.
There were a lot of like custom solutions that had to be built by our product team for them as well.
So I was basically the middleman where I had to like, you know, understand their problems, really, really like sit down with them and be like, hey, how's it going?
What can we do to improve?
Just getting all these client feedback.
So once that was done, um,

I worked closely with the product team just to make sure this feedback was done and making sure, like, timelines were communicated clearly to the client and be like, in a month, you can expect this to, you know, be pushed out, rolled out on the platform so that you would be able to utilize the platform better.
We did a lot of stuff with, like, custom JS scripts for them as well, like, all these JavaScripts in order to crawl the site better.
So actually, just a little bit of backstory, or not backstory, but a little bit of history on, like, my previous company is we were, like, a cloud-based crawler as well.
So if you're in the SEO space, know, like Screaming Frog, that kind of thing, where we crawl clients' websites just to get all, like, their pages.
So we were a cloud-based crawler for enterprise clients.

And so instead of, like, Screaming Frog, where you can crawl, like, a couple of thousand pages, we were able to crawl, like, millions of pages because, you know, it's, like, for enterprise clients, basically.
So we had a lot of, like, custom JS solutions within the platform as well, so we could, like,

Really crawl your site better, get better insights into it, and all this was done, yeah, with the help of my product team, where I was the middleman just going in between, like, the two client, the client and the product.
Yeah.
Awesome.
And in terms of, like, building custom solutions, I'm curious how you thought about, like, where that was worth it.
And in terms of, like, cost-benefit analysis, or how you or the organization made that decision that it was worthy to build a custom solution for a client.
Yeah, definitely.
So I would say, like, they are an enterprise client, so they were paying quite a bit of money.
So this was Macy's, where they had, like, you know, they had a lot of money, I would say.
So they could pay for it, I would say.
And also, in terms of, like, building the custom solutions, they were simple enough that it wasn't, like...
I mean, everything has to be time-based, right?
know, like for our team as well, just to make sure that they're logging the hours and, you know, they're not putting too much hours into it.
I helped out a bit with that as well.
So I have some very tiny experience in like coding.
I could help like generate like the skeleton of the script and then they could like run tests against that script just to make sure that it works.
So I tried my best to make sure that they weren't over like, you know, overworking as well.
That seemed to have worked.
But yeah, they were an important client, especially because they've been with my company for about like, my previous company for about like four years and we didn't want them to churn.
So, you know, it's just like making sure that we have that client like loyalty and really taking into account like their needs and what they want and making sure they are heard.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, we certainly like if they're going to pay for it and then the appetite and it's a...
A name like that, I can understand justifying it.
I think it's also because we wanted to make sure we could upsell them as well in the future.
So it's more like a proof of concept, like, hey, we can do this for you.
You might have to pay a bit more in the future.
And then we did it, and then our account managers managed to negotiate something, and we did upsell them a bit for those solutions.
So it was a great ending to that.
That works, yeah.
If you can justify it.
Cool.
Can you talk to me about your, how you think about organizing your time across clients and prioritize across different deliverables?
Yeah, for sure.
So I usually, when I was at Botify as well, I worked with, like, I think I had, like, 20 clients.
You did say, like, this one had only, like, five clients, but I'm assuming it's a lot more intensive, a lot more hands-on.
Yeah.
We talk to all of them every week.
Yeah.
So I would definitely say I would organize it just in terms of how, how would I say, like how much of at risk the client is of churning.

So definitely, you know, if they have a risk of churning, I would definitely like pour more time into it just to make sure that we are really just aligning the strategy to like what they're expecting.

If that's not the case, where, you know, they're all like an equal level, I would definitely say that based on ARR, I would just make sure that, you I'm attending to like the bigger accounts more attentively before like, you know, moving to like smaller accounts.
Not to say that they don't matter, but big accounts, you know, the ARR is higher, the risk is higher there.

And just having to make sure that like just making sure that, you know, we are reaching out to like not just like one party and like multiple people in the organization so you can expand the network as well.
Just in case someone leaves, you know, that happens a lot.
But yeah, in terms of, like, splitting time, would say, yeah, definitely the bigger accounts first, moving to the smaller accounts, in terms of, like, my time as well.
I would say I'm, like, pretty organized in terms of, like, time management, just making sure that, you know, like, clients' needs are met, and, like, work is handed in on time.
Yeah, I mean, for most of my clients previously, we would meet them on a bi-weekly basis.
It's not, yeah, not as bad, like, not weekly, but I think the most I've met is, like, bi-weekly.
Most of them are, like, monthly basis, so it's not as high of a touchpoint, I would say.
But yeah, definitely having to handle 20 clients, you have to be a bit organized with, like, the work that you're doing as well.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, that makes sense.
Cool.
Well, those are all my questions.
Do you, what questions do you have for me?
Yeah, no, I just wanted to know.
I guess like a bit more about the company.
know it's like an agency structure.
You did say that there was an internal tool as well.
Like what kind of AI workflows are, I guess, if you could expand a bit more on like the AI workflows that the company has.
I tried doing a bit of research into it, but then I couldn't really find too much online.
I was looking through the solutions like.
Yeah.
I would say like the biggest product competitor to what we've built, which is only available like to our internal team, is Aerox.

So if you're familiar with Aerox and building kind of like workflows where it's like step by step, you can have a human in the loop at different stages and kind of like check the execution of each step before continuing.
That's the most similar.
We actually, when we started the company, we were using Aerox and then Marcel and his co-founder decided that like they wanted a lot more customization.
And so we built our own platform.

And the idea is that, like, as we're developing the sort of content and doing working in an agency model, we're also, like, refining the product itself so that eventually, hopefully, like, in the next few months, probably early 2026, although who knows?
You know how product development goes?
We would be able to surface that product externally as a SaaS offering.
So clients that want kind of the full service white glove experience can continue to work with us in the way that we're in our offering today.
But also, like, clients that might have tighter budgets or just want to, like, do this more in-house and own the process could also engage with the product.
But, yeah, if you're looking for a comparison, I would say Aerox is probably the closest to what we're doing internally.
Cool.
In terms of, I guess, the role itself, as an account strategist, would say, how much SEO knowledge?
Are you looking for someone who has a lot of SEO knowledge?
Or is it like they can be fresh?
Or what's more important, I guess, like SEO?
is it like the CSM side of things you'd say for this role?
I would say the CSM side of things, but like SEO is like a huge bonus for us, at least in my mind.

So, you know, the ability to jump in and support if we need to like do a strategy shift or think more creatively about kind of coming up with new keywords or a new approach to the content, like having somebody that knows how to do that is hugely helpful.
The directors are kind of like the ultimately responsible for that.
But I think like, if the account strategist has the ability to like step in and
And like offer some of that, it makes, it just adds to like the trusted portion of the relationship with the client.
Awesome.
For the account strategist role, what kind of KPIs are there?
Is it like kind of like renewal-based, like sales-based, or is it like, yeah, right?
Yeah, so you're not, we don't have like a quota, but it is, we like, we're very focused on retention.
I feel like we just had our one year, hit our one year anniversary of like existing as a company.
So we're still pretty new and it's an open question.
Like all of our investors are kind of like, is everybody renewing?
What does that look like?
Our additional work streams outside of like traditional, that content production, like editorial type of content production are pretty limited.
Like we can offer PSEO as an add-on.
We're like playing around with newsletters and stuff.
So I think helping define what those additional work streams are.
So
Is going to be probably part of what an account strategist starts to do.
But today, like, it's just renewal.
And we sent out our first CSAT survey last week.
So, like, we might probably start, like, getting that continued gauge.
But, yeah, like, and then we'll, we are starting customer health scoring.
So, like, content audits and things like that.
But that's obviously, like, not just on the account strategist.
So just, like, renewals is our P0 right now in retention.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, that's good to know.
Definitely something that I'm familiar with as well.
My KPI for, like, the last four years was just, like, client retention.
Yeah.
sure that we have a healthy retention rate every quarter.
So, cool.
Yeah, I think, I guess, last question.
I know you're here about to jump, but.
No, got 10 minutes.
We're good.
Yeah, kind of like.
Yeah,
I guess, like, what do you like about, like, the company the most?
Like, what do you like working there?
What do you like about working there, you know?
I have never been at a company where, like, that has a waitlist for clients to start working with us.
That's kind of insane to think about.
Like, we were in a call the other day, and my manager told, like, our AE, we only have one account executive.
And she was like, just, like, sometimes don't respond to people for three days.
Like, because, like, we can't handle it.
We're just growing so fast, which is, I mean, that's crazy to see.

And I think I really am a fan of the way that we have built out, like, that agency, the agency is able to kind of sustain the investments that we're making in the product, and also, like, makes us, it forces us to be our own, to be guinea pigs, right?
We have to, like, test.
Test the product and make sure that it's working and have confidence that it's going to be good enough before we put it out.
Like hopefully the MVP will be really a lot more robust because of it.
But like we're not just reliant on like investor money.
We're like essentially breaking even right now in terms of our investment in the product.
And like taking in, you know, clients paying for our services.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Like, I guess like how the how's the culture there like right now in terms of like, I don't know, like office stuff or, you know, meetups or like what's the culture there like right now?
I mean, we're all remote.
So it is that remote aspect of like, you know, we've got like the water cooler channels and the, interest.
Like channels.
And people are pretty active in those.
But we are not incredibly like, yeah, there's not a lot of like, everybody get together in person.
You know, we do we have a company all hands today, we have them bi weekly.
And so I think there's like a lot of visibility into what's happening across the org.
Like, I think one of the things that I try hardest to do is make sure that there's that visibility of like, what other people are working on and things like that.
But the, you know, it's also like, we're growing so fast, and it is like, very fast paced.
And sometimes we don't have enough resources.
So I will say, like, I love the team, because they're incredibly scrappy.
But you have to be really scrappy.
sometimes like, I'm hiring as fast as I can, but we are also like continue to bring on clients.
And so you kind of have to be willing.

To say like, okay, it might be bad for these next two weeks while like our next batch of people on board, but then we'll have more people and like, but that's not because, you know, it's not because we're scaling down and we don't have enough money.
You know, it's not the other side of that problem.
It's because we're going so fast.
For sure.
I definitely get that.
When I started at my previous company, I was the first hire in APAC because they were trying to expand to APAC.
So I definitely get that scrappy mentality and like having to wear different hats as well because I was doing sales at one point and I don't usually do sales.
Like it's like a bit of like a mix of everything.
So yeah, that's definitely really exciting.

But yeah, I think like the U.S., that U.S.
time is going to be pretty, we're going to want to stress that pretty heavily.

right now.

cool.
